summary: I grew up in Lynch, Kentucky, which is located in the northeast part of Harlan County. Lynch was built by U.S. Steel starting in 1917, and it was a coal mining town with a strong sense of community. The town had segregated but equal schools and housing for Black and white residents, and the union helped maintain harmony among workers. I attended the Lynch Colored Public School, which was owned by U.S. Steel, and lived in one of the camps where people of different races and ethnicities lived together within their community.

Harlan County, Kentucky, is home to several coal camps including Lynch and Benham, where I lived and worked. The county was a hub for coal mining operations run by big companies like U.S. Steel and International Harvester. It was a place where diverse groups, including Black, white, and Eastern European immigrants, came together to work in the mines and form tight-knit communities. Despite segregation, the union and company policies promoted harmony so that everyone could live and work together in Harlan County.

Benham, Kentucky, was another coal camp in Harlan County, close to Lynch. While I lived mostly in Lynch, I had connections to Benham, especially through my first wife who was from there. The Benham police were stricter about who could enter the town, especially people from Lynch, but there was some socializing and dating between the towns over time. Benham was part of the larger coal mining community where families often had members working in different camps but maintained close ties.

International Harvester was one of the companies operating coal mines in Harlan County, specifically in Benham. Some miners from Alabama, including family members of mine, worked there. The company was part of the coal mining industry that recruited workers from the South and Europe to staff the mines. It was typical for families to have members working at both International Harvester and U.S. Steel mines in the area.

U.S. Steel was the company that built Lynch, Kentucky, starting in 1917, and it owned the mines and schools there. They set up the entire town, including housing, schools, and mines, and recruited workers from the South and Europe. U.S. Steel promoted union harmony and equal pay scales for Black and white miners, which helped create a relatively peaceful community despite segregation. I attended the Lynch Colored Public School, which was owned by U.S. Steel, and worked in the mines under their operation for 35 years.

I was born in Chambers County, Alabama, in 1930 and came to Kentucky as a child when I was about twelve years old. My father had come a year or two earlier to work in the coal mines and then sent for the rest of the family. Chambers County was a place where many African American families were sharecroppers before moving north to work in the coal mines of Kentucky. This migration was common as companies recruited workers from the South during that time.

Cumberland, Kentucky, was a nearby town to Lynch and Benham where we often went to socialize because it was more neutral territory. Unlike Benham, which had stricter policing, Cumberland allowed people from Lynch to come to its stores, clubs, churches, and boarding houses without much trouble. The Corlee Theatre in Cumberland accepted Black patrons, though they had to sit upstairs, which was better than some other places. Cumberland was an important social hub for the coal mining communities in Harlan County.

The Novo Theatre was a movie theater in the area that, early on, did not accept Black patrons, so we couldn't go there. It was part of the segregation practices in the coal towns, unlike the Corlee Theatre in Cumberland which allowed Black people to attend but required them to sit upstairs. Over time, some of these restrictions eased, but initially, the Novo Theatre was off-limits to us. It symbolized some of the racial barriers that existed despite the overall harmony in the mining communities.

The Corlee Theatre was located in Cumberland, Kentucky, and was more welcoming to Black patrons than other theaters like the Novo. We could attend the Corlee Theatre, but we had to sit upstairs while the white patrons sat downstairs. This was part of the segregation system, but it still allowed us some access to entertainment. Cumberland and its Corlee Theatre served as a social outlet for the Black community in the coal mining region.

One of my sons graduated from East Tennessee State University and was going to be a teacher. It was part of the educational opportunities I wanted for my children, something I didn't have myself. Sending my kids to college was very important to me after working in the mines for 35 years. East Tennessee State University was one of the places where my family advanced beyond the coal mining roots.

Another of my sons graduated from the University of Kentucky, where he earned his undergraduate degree before going on to medical school. This was a big achievement for my family, considering I never had the chance to attend college myself. The University of Kentucky represented a step forward for my children to have professional careers beyond the mines. It showed how far our family had come since our days in Lynch and Harlan County.

My son also attended the University of Louisville to obtain his medical degree. This was part of the educational path that allowed him to become a doctor, which was a source of pride for me. Coming from a mining family in Lynch, Kentucky, it was a significant accomplishment to have children graduate from universities like Louisville. It reflected the opportunities I wanted to provide after working hard in the coal mines for many years.

My son now works in Fort Wayne, Indiana, at one of the biggest doctor's offices there. This shows how far our family has spread from our roots in Lynch, Kentucky. After growing up in a coal mining town and working in the mines myself, it is rewarding to see my children succeed and move to places like Fort Wayne for their careers. It represents the next generation's success beyond the coal fields.

Although I didn't attend college myself, I was aware of institutions like Hampton University as part of the broader educational opportunities for African Americans. My children had the chance to attend universities, and Hampton was one of the historically Black colleges that represented hope and progress for our community. It was important for me to encourage education as a way for my family to move beyond the mining life I knew.
